trajectory_summary: Parker converted a recycled lead from 'five to six months out' to a Tuesday morning appointment in under six minutes — a real outcome. The bill-swap reframe at 01:42 was the call's turning point. However, the booking is structurally fragile: the partner was never confirmed as a required participant, the five-to-six-month timeline objection was never resolved (only bypassed), and the button-up was thin — no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no text follow-up promise.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, which is a real win on a recycled lead that opened with a five-to-six-month deferral. However, the appointment is conditionally fragile: the partner is unconfirmed, the timeline objection was bypassed not resolved, and the button-up is incomplete. The outcome should be tagged 'booked — fragile' rather than a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: No. It's just something I'm I'm gonna have to discuss with my my other half.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker's instinct was right — he referenced 'you and your partner' at 03:47 — but he never asked 'What time works for both of you?' The partner's availability was never confirmed as a condition of the booking. On a recycled lead where the prior appointment may have already fallen through once, an unconfirmed partner is the most likely no-show vector. The codex template ('What time works for both of you to be on the call together?') is one sentence that converts Parker's good intent into a protected sit.

key_moments.quote: I was just reaching out because it looks like we built up a solar design for you that we weren't able to go to quite yet. Does that sound familiar?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker's opener explicitly references a prior design build and prior contact — this is a recycled lead signal. The call never collected a utility bill, which looks like a collection gap. But on a recycled lead, Ion may already have Andy's bill on file from the prior touch. The coaching implication depends on whether the bill was already captured.

key_moments.quote: I will say just before you kinda dismiss it completely, I know solar in the past kinda wasn't an extra bill. And, to be honest with you though, now what we do is we do a bill swap. So instead of adding another bill to your plate, we're taking away your electric bill, and we're adding that solar bill, so we're swapping it.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's best moment. Parker correctly identified Andy's 'another bill' framing as an inversion opportunity and executed a bill-swap reframe without being prompted. The move is schema-aligned — it flips the cost-addition frame into a cost-replacement frame. Andy's 'Okay' at 02:13 confirms it landed. The execution is slightly soft ('kinda wasn't an extra bill' is imprecise) but the strategy is right.

key_moments.quote: So right now, we're probably looking at at least about five to six months down the road before we even can discuss anything like that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Andy gives Parker an explicit timeline objection — five to six months — which is a flip opportunity. Parker's bill-swap reframe addressed the 'another bill' framing but never touched the timeline itself. The five-to-six-month deferral was bypassed, not resolved. This is a reactive gap: Parker moved to pitch without inverting the timeline.

key_moments.quote: No. It's just something I'm I'm gonna have to discuss with my my other half.
key_moments.stewart_read: Andy raises the spouse signal clearly. Parker's intent appears inclusion-directed — at 03:47 he says 'we could call you and your partner' — but his execution never confirms the partner as a hard condition for the appointment. He uses calendar urgency ('our calendar does fill pretty quickly') to push toward booking rather than anchoring the partner's presence as the reason to book. The distinction matters: Parker's language frames the partner as someone Andy can 'double check with' after booking, not as a required participant whose availability should gate the time slot.
